results.title: SellerClaw: A team of AI agents that runs your stores across channels | Product Hunt
results.content:
The human-in-the-loop part is what I kept coming back to. The ability to set how much runs autonomously vs. what needs your approval before it fires means you can start in advisory mode and only expand the automation as trust builds. That's actually how I'd want to hand anything over in my own workflow. No "rogue" pricing changes showing up while I was asleep.

Still early product, so the multi-channel coverage is limited to Shopify and eBay for now. If you're on Etsy or managing Amazon FBA in parallel, you'll need to wait on that. But for what it does today, especially for solo operators who need more bandwidth without the hiring cost, the architecture is genuinely solid.

#### What needs improvement [...] Our approach is to keep the agent decision-making layer separate from the source-of-truth transaction layer: the agent can decide and act, but the final numbers, inventory state, order status, and settlement always come from the platform or connected system itself. So the promise is only made off live data, and every action has to reconcile back to the system that will actually fulfill it.

Report

Florent Berrez

Foyer

Foyer [...] Ease of use

Reliability

Value for money

Customization

Report

 94 views

•1 review

#### What's great

SellerClaw stands out because it actually helps run the business, not just analyze it. A lot of AI tools are good for research or sourcing, but this feels much more like an operations layer for Shopify. I also really like that when researching new product opportunities, it can pull signals from social media trends on TikTok and Instagram through SocialVault. That’s a big deal for me as a dropshipper, since social ads are my main traffic source and trend data matters a lot.

#### What needs improvement

results.title: Fakespot Competitors & Alternatives (2026) | Product Hunt
results.content:
In evaluating these options, we looked at what each tool optimizes for (trust signals vs research speed vs savings), how well it fits into real shopping workflows via browser extensions, and the breadth of coverage beyond a single store. We also weighed the clarity and explainability of insights, practical reliability (e.g., page-scanning approaches that may be brittle), and whether features like comparisons, multi-source pricing, and alerts meaningfully reduce decision effort.

## BuyScout® AI Shopping Assistant

BuyScout® AI Shopping AssistantBuyScout® AI Shopping Assistant

Your AI Copilot for Online Shopping

4.5 · 2 reviews

Learn more → [...] 5.0 · 1 review

Learn more →

Reverse Dropshipping targets a different shopping risk than Fakespot: paying too much because an item is simply a marked-up dropshipped product. Instead of analyzing review trust, it helps identify when a listing may be a resold commodity item and points shoppers toward the original source.

This makes it especially valuable when the suspicion isn’t fake reviews, but inflated pricing and unclear provenance. It can also help buyers compare against cheaper equivalents and avoid impulse purchases driven by branding, packaging, or storefront presentation. [...] Wisely is also built around lightweight, in-browser usage, so it fits naturally into window-shopping workflows. Because it relies on page extraction rather than deep marketplace integrations, it’s best for users who want speed and convenience over a platform-level trust model.

If the main pain is time spent reading reviews, Wisely can replace dozens of scrolls with a few clear takeaways.

### Best for

Ideal for Amazon shoppers who want fast, review-based pros and cons.

### Standout features

 ✓Pros and cons extracted from reviews
 ✓Quick issue and shortcoming highlights
 ✓Amazon-focused browser extension workflow
 ✓Concise product insights while browsing

results.title: Firecoach AI: AI roleplays that turn reps into top performers | Product Hunt
results.content:
Report

Jim Jeffers

That pattern-map example is exactly the trust layer I was hoping existed. It turns “clone the top performer” into something much more precise: copy the move only where the evidence says it travels.

The one UX detail I’d be careful with is making the boundary visible to reps, not just managers. If a rep can see “this move works in discovery for mid-market, but not in enterprise pricing,” the coaching feels less like a black-box grade and more like judgment they can actually learn.

Report

Jérôme

Nice idea, would be very beneficial for my team as well. What CRM can you integrate? I'm assuming SalesForce, how about SAP CRM?

Report

Midori Verity

Firecoach AI

Firecoach AI

Maker [...] Reps performing to the scorecard instead of to the buyer is real, especially when the scorecard is generic. We hit this hard during early testing (one of our clients caught his AI penalizing reps for objections the buyer never raised), and we rebuilt the whole scoring layer around it.

A few things we did to avoid the trap:

→ Scorecards are tweaked based on YOUR top performer's actual moves, not a generic methodology. "Sounding like the methodology" only counts if the methodology is literally what your best closer does.

→ Manager in the loop by default. Sales leaders edit scoring criteria and can override individual scoring decisions. AI assists. It doesn't judge.

results.title: We're live. Here's what you're telling us, what's next ... - Product Hunt
results.content:
The critique worth repeating

One of you asked the question we ask ourselves. Isn't a trust badge just the next thing to get gamed? Fair. We are not pretending we end the arms race. What we can do is raise the cost of cheating high enough that it stops being worth it for most bad actors, and keep moving faster than they do. If you have ideas on how to make that harder to game, that door is wide open.

What is next

Much of this is getting shaped today in real time:

Review authenticity scoring, so you can see which reviews are actually real.

A weekly digest of the fake discounts we caught, including ones you report.

Landed cost, so shipping is baked into whether the "cheaper" option is actually cheaper. [...] What is landing

The feature you keep calling out is Trust This Price. Dozens of you shared the same story in different words. You caught a "sale" that was just the normal price dressed up, sometimes sitting at that level for three months straight. That is the exact moment we built Dupely for, and watching it happen in the wild today made our week.

What you are asking most

By a wide margin, the top question is how DupeScore actually decides two listings are the same product and not just similar, especially when a seller changes the bundle size, repackages, or renames the item to dodge comparison. [...] Short version:we do not score on the title. We compare the underlying signals, images, descriptions, and specs together, and we factor pack size and quantity directly. So a rename alone will not fool the score, and two listings with different quantities will not get called a true match even if everything else lines up. The fake-sale flag works the same way, baselining against 90 days of real price history instead of whatever label the listing is wearing today.

A few of you also asked how seller credibility gets verified. A brand-new or overnight shop usually has not built enough signal yet, ratings, return volume, tenure, so that thin history becomes a flag in itself. We are refining this as more sellers move through the system.

The critique worth repeating
